key_departures.impact: Three-year starter at left guard for Pittsburgh, strong PFF grades (73.3 in 2025). Signed with Cardinals on a 3yr/$31.5M deal — a raise from his Pittsburgh contract. Leaves a genuine hole at LG with no clear in-house replacement beyond Spencer Anderson.

Team MVP award winner in 2025 — a receiving-back who was the centerpiece of the Pittsburgh offense. His departure was a significant loss, particularly for the passing game. Replaced by Rico Dowdle, but Dowdle is a different profile (power runner vs. pass-catching back).

Stepped into a starting role in 2025 after Darius Slay's departure, played solidly. Departure leaves CB depth thinner, but Pittsburgh addressed CB2/CB3 directly via Jamel Dean and Asante Samuel Jr. re-signing.
Primary punt returner and WR3/4. His departure leaves a legitimate special teams hole — no clear punt returner on the roster. Also removes WR depth behind Metcalf and Pittman.

Valuable F-back/TE hybrid and core special teams player. Departure removes a versatile piece and further thins special teams. With Jonnu Smith released, TE depth is now razor thin behind Pat Freiermuth and Darnell Washington.

Special teams captain who suffered a season-ending injury in Week 5 of 2025. Departure alongside Heyward, Pierre, Austin, and Waitman represents a massive special teams overhaul. Net impact on defense is minimal.

Released by Pittsburgh after a disappointing single season (38 receptions, 5.8 ypc). Was acquired via trade with Miami in 2025. Departure was expected given poor production. Leaves TE depth thin behind Freiermuth/Washington.

Rodgers quarterbacked Pittsburgh to a 10-7 record and AFC North title in 2025 but has been non-committal about returning. GM Khan has said the door is open. Without Rodgers, Pittsburgh has only Mason Rudolph and 2025 6th-round pick Will Howard. QB remains the biggest unresolved need on the roster.
Starting punter in 2025 (45.5 ypp avg). Departure replaced by Cameron Johnston signing. Special teams concern given multiple departures.
Veteran WR retired in January 2026. Was a depth piece and locker room presence. Minimal on-field impact given age and role.

signings.trade_details: PIT sent 2026 6th-round pick (#214) to IND; received Pittman + 2026 7th-round pick (#230)

contract.notes: OTC reports 3yr/$70M total contract with $41M guaranteed. Extension replaces remaining year of prior Colts deal. ESPN reported $59M extension figure; OTC reflects combined contract value. AAV of $19.67M per season per multiple sources.

signings.analysis: Fully addresses the WR2 need. Pittman (80 catches, 784 yds, 7 TDs in 2025) gives Pittsburgh a legitimate second receiver alongside DK Metcalf. Three-year commitment with significant guarantees signals this is a real solution, not a bridge. The combined AAV of Metcalf and Pittman is $52.7M, 5th-most for any WR1/WR2 tandem in the NFL. WR need drops from a tier 1 need to off the board entirely. Depth still needed, but starter-level need is resolved.

contract.notes: Per OTC and Spotrac. Only the $12M signing bonus is guaranteed. $4M roster bonus due 3rd day of 2027 league year — creates Year 2 decision point. Low 2026 cap charge of $5.5M due to front-loaded signing bonus structure.

signings.analysis: Three-year deal secures a starting CB2 alongside Joey Porter Jr. Dean had 3 INTs, 14 starts, and 9 PDs in 2025 with Tampa Bay. Contract is team-friendly with an escape hatch after Year 1 if needed ($8M dead cap). The combination of Dean (3yr) + Asante Samuel Jr. re-signing + Porter Jr. (incumbent) means CB is no longer a meaningful draft need. Need fully addressed at starter level.

contract.notes: Per Spotrac. Guarantees not separately broken out publicly; estimate is approximately half of total value.

signings.analysis: Dowdle is coming off back-to-back 1,000-yard rushing seasons (1,076 yards, 6 TDs in 2025) and has familiarity with Mike McCarthy's scheme from Dallas. Two-year deal at mid-tier RB money signals a real commitment. However, Dowdle is a different profile than Kenneth Gainwell (power runner vs. pass-catching back) — Gainwell was the team MVP specifically for receiving contributions. The receiving-back need is not fully solved. Pairs with Jaylen Warren and 2025 3rd-round pick Kaleb Johnson. RB need drops from tier 1 (critical) to tier 3 (depth/receiving specialist) because rushing is covered but the pass-catching void remains.

contract.notes: Per Spotrac and multiple reports. Exact guarantee not publicly confirmed; estimated at approximately half of total value typical for one-year deals.

signings.analysis: Brisker started all 17 games for the Bears in 2025 despite a history of concussions. One-year deal at $5.5M is below starter-tier AAV ($10M+) for safety, but this is a prove-it deal for a player returning from injury history. Pittsburgh-native adds motivation factor. Slots in as a primary starting safety alongside DeShon Elliott. However, one-year structure signals this is a bridge — the team still needs a long-term safety answer. Safety need drops from tier 1 to tier 2 given the one-year contract structure and health concerns.

contract.notes: Per ESPN, OTC, and Spotrac. Total two-year value of $32.25M with $16.25M fully guaranteed. Year 1 (2026) fully guaranteed. Also creates $5.5M in cap space by restructuring. Largest contract ever given to an NFL defensive player age 36 or older per Ian Rapoport.

signings.analysis: Re-signing/extension keeps the heart of the defensive line in Pittsburgh through 2027. Heyward was 2nd-team AP All-Pro in 2025 (3.5 sacks, 78 tackles). This was not a need-filling move — Heyward was already under contract — but the extension creates $5.5M in additional cap flexibility and eliminates any impending free agency uncertainty. IDL was never a primary need given Heyward + Keeanu Benton + Derrick Harmon.

contract.notes: Per ESPN (Adam Schefter) and Steelers Depot. $6M in Year 1 (2026). Steelers Depot noted 2026 cap charge of approximately $3.65M.

signings.analysis: Two-year deal adds interior line depth and run-stopping versatility — Joseph-Day has experience at three-tech and nose tackle. Expected to rotate behind Heyward and Derrick Harmon. Two-year deal with $6M year-one cash is a real commitment for a depth/rotational piece. Addresses the IDL depth concern (multiple injuries to rotational players in 2025) but doesn't elevate him above starter level. IDL need — already minor — is further reduced.

contract.notes: Per ESPN's Jeremy Fowler and Still Curtain. One-year, $4M deal. Returning from spinal fusion surgery in 2025.

signings.analysis: Samuel joined mid-season in 2025 and started 3 games, flashing upside before his injury history. One-year deal is explicitly a prove-it arrangement. At $4M and one year, this is a depth/developmental signing rather than a solution. Works in tandem with Jamel Dean to fortify the CB room. CB need is already addressed by Dean; this adds competition and insurance.

contract.notes: Per Ari Meirov/MySportsUpdate and CBS Pittsburgh. Two-year deal with approximately $5M max value. OTC notes 2026 cap charge of approximately $2M. Signing bonus of $625K.

signings.analysis: Two-year retention of a versatile LB/special teams contributor. Holcomb appeared in 22 games over 2 seasons with Pittsburgh including 11 starts. Low-cost, high-loyalty signing that provides familiarity with the defense under a new coaching staff. Does not materially move the needle on LB as a positional need — Patrick Queen departed last offseason and the starting LB picture still relies on younger pieces.

contract.notes: Per OTC via Steelers Depot. Veteran benefit contract. Base salary $1.3M, signing bonus $187,500. Discounted cap charge of approximately $1.26M.

signings.analysis: Former first-round pick (2019, Packers) on a near-veteran-minimum contract. Savage split 2025 between three teams and was not a full-time starter. Versatile (plays both FS and SS) and has ball skills (10 career INTs) but is clearly depth behind Brisker and DeShon Elliott. Veteran benefit contract structure = camp body/emergency piece. Does not address the safety need meaningfully — just adds roster depth and familiarity (former Packers connection to new coaching staff).

contract.notes: Contract terms not officially confirmed; one-year deal per CBS Pittsburgh and ESPN. Estimated value based on prior contract and market.

signings.analysis: One-year retention of a rotational IDL who appeared in 12 games in 2025 (9 tackles, 1 sack). Provides continuity in the defensive line rotation. Low-cost depth move; does not change any positional need calculus.

contract.notes: One-year deal per CBS Pittsburgh and ESPN. Terms not fully confirmed publicly; estimated value.

signings.analysis: Directly replaces departing Corliss Waitman. Johnston has prior familiarity with the Steelers organization (signed in 2024, lost to injury in Week 1) and worked with kicker Chris Boswell as his holder. Punter vacancy created by Waitman's departure is filled. Not a draft/free agency priority going forward.

contract.notes: One-year deal per CBS Pittsburgh. Contract terms not publicly confirmed; estimated at league minimum range.

signings.analysis: Driscoll spent time with the Steelers in 2025 but did not appear in a game. Re-signing is an insurance move. Does not address the starting left guard vacancy created by Isaac Seumalo's departure to Arizona. IOL remains a significant need.

recommended_need_adjustments.reasoning: Aaron Rodgers remains unsigned and uncommitted. Pittsburgh has only Mason Rudolph and 2025 6th-round pick Will Howard under contract. If Rodgers returns, this need drops sharply to tier 4/5. But as of March 16, QB is the single largest unresolved need on the roster and must remain tier 1 until a decision is made. Draft should reflect this — strongly consider QB in rounds 1-3 regardless of Rodgers outcome.

Isaac Seumalo's departure (3yr/$31.5M to Arizona) creates a genuine starting left guard vacancy. No meaningful replacement has been signed — Jack Driscoll (re-signed, never played a snap in 2025) is the only in-house option. Spencer Anderson is the presumed internal candidate but is unproven as a starter. This is a new need that escalated from low-concern to critical. Should target IOL in rounds 1-3 of the draft or remaining free agency market.

Michael Pittman Jr. trade + 3yr/$59M extension gives Pittsburgh a legitimate WR1/WR2 tandem with DK Metcalf for the first time in years. WR starter need is fully resolved. Calvin Austin III's departure leaves a depth/returner hole, but that is a day 3 or street FA fix, not a premium draft priority. WR drops from tier 2 to tier 4 (depth only).

Jamel Dean (3yr/$36.75M) slots in as CB2 opposite Joey Porter Jr., and Asante Samuel Jr. re-signed (1yr/$4M) adds CB3/nickel competition. James Pierre's departure is fully offset. The CB room is now one of the deepest in the AFC. CB drops from a meaningful need to a luxury/depth consideration only. Teams should note Dean's one-year escape hatch if they want a long-term developmental CB, but it is not urgent.

Rico Dowdle (2yr/$12.25M) addresses the power-running vacancy but does not replicate Kenneth Gainwell's value as a pass-catching back — the specific role for which Gainwell won team MVP. The receiving-back role remains unfilled. Jaylen Warren and Kaleb Johnson add depth, but a pass-catching back remains a real need. RB stays at tier 3 — the rushing game is addressed but the receiving RB hole persists.

Jaquan Brisker (1yr/$5.5M) provides a starting-caliber safety but is on a prove-it deal due to concussion history. Darnell Savage adds depth at minimum cost. However, neither signing represents a long-term solution — Brisker will be a free agent again after 2026, and multiple safety starters from 2025 (Dugger, Peppers, Clark) have departed. Safety needs a long-term answer in the draft (rounds 1-3 given the strength of the 2026 safety class). Stays at tier 2.

Connor Heyward departed (Raiders) and Jonnu Smith was released, stripping the TE room to Pat Freiermuth and Darnell Washington. No TE has been added in free agency. Pittsburgh still has capable starters at the position in Freiermuth/Washington, but depth is critically thin and the F-back/move-TE role Heyward filled is vacant. TE holds at tier 3 — not a top-of-draft priority but needs to be addressed in mid-rounds.
No movement at OT in free agency. Broderick Jones remains the incumbent LT. Calvin Anderson was released, thinning depth further. OT remains a developmental need heading into the draft.

Cameron Heyward's extension (2yr/$32.25M) + Sebastian Joseph-Day (2yr/$11M) + Esezi Otomewo re-sign + Derrick Harmon + Keeanu Benton gives Pittsburgh a deep and capable IDL group. The rotational injury issue of 2025 is addressed. IDL can drop to tier 4 — depth is fine, starter quality is elite with Heyward. Not a draft priority.

key_additions.headline: Physical WR2 (80 catches, 7 TDs in 2025) pairs with DK Metcalf, finally giving Pittsburgh a legitimate receiving duo; acquired for a mere 6th-for-7th pick swap
Second-biggest free agent deal in Steelers history fills CB2 slot opposite Joey Porter Jr.; Dean had 3 INTs and 9 PDs in 14 starts in 2025
Back-to-back 1,000-yard rusher reunites with head coach Mike McCarthy (shared time in Dallas) to replace departing team MVP Kenneth Gainwell
Pittsburgh native and Penn State product returns home on a prove-it deal; started all 17 regular-season games in 2025 for Chicago
2025 2nd-team All-Pro re-signs on historic deal (largest ever for an NFL defensive player age 36+), anchoring Pittsburgh's defense through 2027
Versatile run-stopper bolsters interior depth behind Heyward and Harmon; 10 starts in 2025 for Tennessee

key_losses.headline: Three-year starter and 2024 Pro Bowl LG departs for a raise in Arizona, creating Pittsburgh's biggest remaining need on the roster
Team MVP departs after becoming Pittsburgh's most reliable receiving weapon in 2025; partially replaced by Dowdle but the pass-catching role is unfilled
42-year-old starter remains unsigned and non-committal about 2026; Pittsburgh has only Mason Rudolph and Will Howard under contract, leaving QB as the franchise's biggest open question
Primary punt returner departs after four seasons, leaving Pittsburgh without a clear return specialist heading into 2026
Cam's younger brother and special teams captain departs after 4 seasons, removing a key F-back piece and over 1,100 career special teams snaps from Pittsburgh's roster

notes: Aaron Rodgers remains an active UFA as of March 16, 2026. GM Khan has confirmed the door is open for a return but no deal has been offered or signed. All other signings listed are confirmed transactions. Michael Pittman Jr.'s exact guarantee breakdown on the new PIT extension is not yet fully confirmed — OTC lists 3yr/$70M total (absorbing remaining Colts deal year) with $41M guaranteed; all sources agree on $59M extension figure from Garafolo/Schefter. Pittman's contract structure notes reflect the OTC figure as most reliable. Cap space post-FA reflects OTC's current published figure of $22.9M.
